title: Osteocyte-Intrinsic MMP13 Exacerbates Injury-Induced Temporomandibular Joint Osteoarthritis
research_question: Does osteocytic MMP13 play a causal role in temporomandibular joint osteoarthritis (TMJOA) progression, similar to its role in knee OA?
methods_summary: Generated DMP1-cre+/-;MMP13fl/fl mice with osteocyte-intrinsic MMP13 deletion
Induced TMJOA via intra-articular monosodium iodoacetate (MIA) injection in 12-week-old male mice
Evaluated TMJ and knee joint pathology using histology (Safranin-O/Fast Green, Ploton silver stain, Picrosirius red, TRAP stain, IHC/IF) and microCT
Assessed cartilage degeneration using Modified TMJ Mankin score
Analyzed subchondral bone parameters including lacunar density, collagen organization, osteoclast activity, and bone mineralization
dataset: 16-week-old male DMP1-cre+/-;MMP13fl/fl mice and Cre-negative littermate controls (mixed FVB and C57BL/6 background); n=6-9 per group for TMJ studies, n=6-11 per group for knee studies
key_findings: Osteocytic MMP13 deficiency protected TMJ from MIA-induced cartilage degeneration and subchondral bone changes, unlike knee where it exacerbated baseline OA
MIA increased osteocyte lacunar density in control TMJs but not in DMP1-cre+/-;MMP13fl/fl mice
Osteocytic MMP13 deficiency prevented injury-induced subchondral bone plate thickening in both TMJ and knee
No baseline TMJ phenotype was observed in DMP1-cre+/-;MMP13fl/fl mice, contrasting with knee OA at baseline
Collagen organization in TMJ subchondral bone was unaffected by genotype or MIA treatment
extracted_insights: Osteocytic MMP13 is required for injury-induced TMJOA progression but not for TMJ homeostasis at baseline, highlighting joint-specific roles of osteocyte-mediated perilacunocanalicular remodeling. MMP13 deficiency protects both TMJ and knee from injury-induced subchondral bone changes, suggesting a common protective mechanism despite divergent baseline phenotypes.
